hook: Roman to medieval Valencia laid bare beneath a glass-floored plaza
facts: Excavated by Valencia's SIAM between 1985 and 1997, the city's largest visitable dig.
Layers run from the 2nd century BC Roman founding to the 14th century.
You can read the crossing of the cardo and decumanus, the forum's curia and basilica.
A shallow reflecting pool roofs the site; the museum sits below ground.
w: Walk over a sheet of water beside the cathedral and the whole Roman town lies underfoot: forum, curia, the cross of cardo and decumanus, then Visigothic and Moorish layers stacked on top. Dug out across 1985 to 1997, it reads like a core sample of the city. Twenty-two centuries, one staircase down.

hook: A baroque cocktail salon dressed like a medieval book of hours
facts: Opened 1994 on Carrer del Conde de Almodovar, off Plaza de la Virgen.
Named for the illuminated medieval 'books of hours'; crammed with paintings and busts.
Pours its own Agua de Valencia, the city's orange-and-cava cocktail.
Candlelit, velvet and gilt; theatrical rather than tasteful.
w: Named for the illuminated medieval books of hours and decorated to match: gilt frames, busts, dripping candles, velvet crammed corner to corner. Since 1994 it has poured a proper Agua de Valencia, the local orange-and-cava bomb. Maximalist, faintly absurd, gloriously committed to the bit. Order the orange thing.

hook: Gothic shell, baroque ceiling, the so-called Sistine Chapel of Valencia
facts: Nearly 1,900 square metres of fresco cover the vault, painted 1690 to 1704.
Designed by Antonio Palomino, executed by his pupil Dionis Vidal.
Cleaned 2016 with help from a former restorer of Rome's Sistine Chapel.
Plain Gothic exterior hides the explosion of colour inside.
w: From a backstreet you would walk past the plain Gothic shell. Inside, nearly 1,900 square metres of baroque fresco boil across the vault, painted 1690 to 1704 by Palomino and his pupil Vidal. Cleaned in 2016 by hands that had worked on Rome's Sistine ceiling. Look up until your neck complains.

hook: An agate cup the Vatican calls a credible candidate for the Last Supper chalice
facts: Housed in the cathedral's old chapter house, a star-vaulted Gothic room.
The relic's agate cup dates to roughly 100 to 50 BC, eastern Mediterranean work.
Reached Valencia in 1437 via King Alfonso the Magnanimous.
Popes John Paul II and Benedict XVI both said Mass with it.
legend: Tradition holds Saint Peter carried the cup from Jerusalem to Rome, then it was hidden in Pyrenean caves during the Moorish invasion.
w: In the cathedral's old chapter house, under a star vault, sits a dark agate cup of about 100 BC that the Vatican rates the most credible Last Supper chalice going. Legend has Saint Peter carrying it from Jerusalem, then monks hiding it in Pyrenean caves. Two popes have said Mass with it. The Grail, plausibly.

a: Plaza Lope de Vega / Ciutat Vella
hook: A house barely a metre wide, said to be Europe's narrowest
facts: Facade measures roughly 107 centimetres across.
Five floors, each one room, once linked by a spiral stair.
Ground floor long held a tiny family jewellers.
Probably built thin to dodge a tax levied by facade width.
w: A facade about 107 centimetres wide, often billed as Europe's narrowest house. Five storeys, one room each, threaded by a spiral stair, with a doll-sized jewellers once on the ground floor. Reputedly built this thin to duck a tax charged by frontage width. Spite, made permanent in stone.

hook: A Christian-era hammam that bathed Valencians for six centuries
facts: Founded around 1313 by permit of King James II.
Cold, warm and hot vaulted rooms pierced by star-shaped skylights.
Mudejar work, built Christian but laid out like an Islamic steam bath.
Stayed open until 1959, then restored in the early 1960s.
w: Built around 1313 under a Christian king but laid out as a hammam: cold, warm and hot vaulted rooms with star-shaped skylights spilling daylight onto the stone. Mudejar craft put to a Muslim purpose. It scrubbed Valencians right up to 1959, six centuries of steam. Then the showers killed it.

n: L'Iber Museo de los Soldaditos de Plomo
hook: A Gothic palace packed with the world's largest army of toy soldiers
facts: Over 95,000 figures on show, more than a million in the collection.
Set in the late-Gothic Palacio de los Marqueses de Malferit on Carrer Caballeros.
Battles, courts and daily life staged in obsessive painted dioramas.
Opened 2007 by the Noguera family's Libertas 7 foundation.
w: Inside a late-Gothic palace on Caballeros, a single family's mania: more than 95,000 lead soldiers on display and over a million in the vaults, the largest such hoard anywhere. Whole battles and royal courts staged in painted dioramas. Opened 2007. Childhood obsession scaled to museum size.

n: Gargoyles of the Lonja de la Seda
hook: A UNESCO Gothic masterpiece studded with lewd, satirical stone monsters
facts: The 15th-century silk exchange became a UNESCO site in 1996.
Its Sala de Contratacion raises twisting columns like stone palm trees.
Some 28 gargoyles mix beasts, sinners and frank obscenity.
The carvings nod to medieval bestiaries and their moral warnings.
w: Everyone gawps at the spiralling palm-tree columns of the trading hall. Walk the cornices instead: among the 28 gargoyles on this UNESCO Gothic gem crouch beasts, drunks relieving themselves and carvings too rude to describe in a guidebook. Medieval bestiary as moral lesson, and as dirty joke.

hook: A Civil War bomb shelter beneath the streets of the old town
facts: Dug in 1937 as Valencia served as Republican capital under air raids.
Three concrete naves with a roof over 1.5 metres thick.
Original wall benches and ventilation survive inside.
One entrance keeps its Art Deco REFUGIO lettering; opened to visitors in 2018.
w: When Valencia became the Republic's capital and Italian bombers came, the city sank shelters under its streets. This one, dug in 1937 at Serranos 25, keeps three concrete naves, wall benches, the ventilation, and over its door the word REFUGIO in Art Deco letters. Opened to visitors in 2018. The fear, preserved.

hook: A walled neoclassical garden of marble statues and clipped mazes
facts: Valencia's last surviving neoclassical historic garden.
Some 33 Carrara marble statues among ponds and low hedge labyrinths.
Two stone lions were carved for Madrid's parliament, then rejected as too small.
Hidden behind a high wall near the old river park.
w: Behind a high wall hides Valencia's last neoclassical garden: 33 Carrara figures posing among ponds and clipped hedge mazes. The two stone lions at the gate were carved for Madrid's parliament, then sent away for being too small. A favourite for wedding photos, ignored by everyone else. Quiet, and a little smug.

hook: A grid of tile-fronted fishermen's cottages by the old beach
facts: Once an independent fishing town, absorbed by Valencia in the late 1800s.
Low two-storey houses sheathed in bright modernista ceramic tiles.
A 2013 survey catalogued 272 tiled houses in the quarter.
Saved from a demolition road plan after years of resident protest.
w: Behind the beach runs a grid of low fishermen's cottages, their fronts sheathed in bright tile, a poor district's homemade answer to Gaudi's mosaics. A 2013 survey counted 272 of them. A road scheme meant to flatten the lot; residents fought it off street by street. Walk Carrer de la Reina and look up.

hook: An eccentric Art Deco tower mixing Egyptian, Arab and Hebrew motifs
facts: Built 1930 on Calle Castellon by architect Juan Guardiola Martinez.
Seven storeys of mashed-up Oriental, Egyptian and Hindu ornament.
Nicknamed the Jewish House for a Star of David over the doorway.
A standout among Ruzafa's run of modernista facades.
w: Locals call it the Jewish House for the Star of David set over the door, though no synagogue ever sat inside. Architect Guardiola raised it in 1930 and crammed the seven storeys with Egyptian columns, Arab arches and Hindu flourishes, all at once. Ruzafa's strangest facade by a street. Look up before the cocktails.
